Daily Digest

20 May 2026 – Today's Key Reads

Curated briefing on energy, economy, agriculture and strategic affairs — with full-text coverage from today's editions.

Wednesday
20 May 2026
Updated 07:30 IST
ENERGY • Shubhangi Mathur • New Delhi

Russia oil waiver extension a huge relief, say experts

In a relief to refiners in India, the United States has extended its waiver from sanctions on purchasing Russian crude oil and petroleum products for a month to temporarily provide "the most vulnerable nations" access to fuel produced in the Eurasian country and stranded at sea.

According to an order by the US Department of the Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), countries are authorised to procure Russian oil loaded on or before April 17. This can be done until June 17.

"This extension will provide additional flexibility and we will work with these nations to provide specific licenses as needed. This general licence will help stabilise the physical crude market and ensure oil reaches the most energy-vulnerable countries," US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a post on X.

Bessent said the 30-day window would help reroute existing supplies to countries most in need, by reducing China's ability to stockpile discounted oil.

The earlier US waiver lapsed on May 16.

A government official said on May 18 that Indian refiners would continue buying crude oil from Russia even in the absence of a US waiver. "I would like to emphasise that we have been purchasing (crude oil) from Russia before and during the waiver. We are buying even now. There should be commercial sense for oil-marketing companies to purchase (Russian oil)," said Sujata Sharma, joint secretary at the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas.

Experts say the US waiver has enabled Indian oil firms to buy crude oil from sanctioned Russian entities, ensuring fuel availability. "Oil companies in India will have to scale back purchases if the US waiver is not renewed, given the payment constraints and other related issues," Prashant Vashisht, senior vice-president, ICRA, had said on May 15.

India has ramped up purchases from Russia since the war started in West Asia. The country bought 1.87 million barrels per day in May so far, meeting approximately 40 per cent of its oil imports, according to Kpler.

Under the US sanctions, countries are allowed to purchase Russian oil from sanctioned entities, including Rosneft and Lukoil. Prior to the sanctions, the two producers supplied around 60 per cent of India's Russian oil purchases.

As supply from traditional West Asian suppliers remains constrained, refiners have increased reliance on Russian crude.

AGRICULTURE • Hemant Kumar Rout • Bhubaneswar

Trying to get fertilisers from 28 nations: Chouhan

Amid supply disruption triggered by the war in West Asia, India is facing difficulties in procuring fertilisers from the global market, Union Agriculture Minister Shivraj Singh Chouhan said on Tuesday. He urged states and farmers to adopt organic farming and drought-resistant crops.

"Fertilisers are not easily available in the international market. The government is trying hard to procure them from wherever they are available," he said. India could procure fertilisers from 28 countries and the Centre was in talks with all of them.

"The global supply situation has become challenging due to geopolitical tensions. Although we have sufficient stocks for the upcoming kharif season, farmers have been requested to reduce the use of chemical fertilisers. They should try natural farming in at least a portion of their farms. States must ensure that subsidised fertilisers are not diverted," he said.

To shield farmers, the Union Cabinet approved ₹41,534 crore for nutrient-based fertiliser subsidies.

Govt restores 97% natural gas supply to fertiliser units: The Centre has restored 97 per cent of natural gas needs through aggressive spot buying, up from 65 per cent at the start of the crisis. Through strategic tendering outside the Strait of Hormuz, India has secured 2.5 million tonnes of urea, 1.35 mt of DAP and 0.7 mt of NPK for May-June.

ECONOMY • Anjali Kumari • Mumbai

RBI likely to hold repo rate at 5.25% through FY27: India Ratings

The Reserve Bank of India is expected to hold the repo rate at 5.25 per cent for the rest of FY27, India Ratings said, noting inflation would stay within tolerance despite higher fuel prices. It pegged GDP growth at 6.7 per cent and retail inflation at 4.4 per cent.

Icra trimmed its FY27 GDP forecast to 6.2 per cent from 6.5 per cent, citing elevated crude prices. It sees growth moderating to 7.0 per cent in Q4 FY26.

In its April review, the RBI projected FY27 GDP growth at 6.9 per cent. "We are seeing an upward trajectory in CPI... we are still expecting CPI to remain within the RBI tolerance band of less than 6 per cent. We don't expect the RBI to increase or reduce the repo rate," India Ratings said.

The West Asian conflict is weighing on India's outlook. Private investments could remain subdued amid tepid demand and uncertainty. Sectors dependent on crude derivatives face pressure, while data centres may provide support.

India Ratings projects the trade deficit at around 10.4 per cent of GDP – the highest in ten years – driven by crude prices and currency depreciation. The rupee is expected to depreciate 6-7 per cent on average in FY27. The 10-year yield is likely to stay above 7 per cent.

It assumes crude at $95 per barrel in FY27 ($110 in Q1). Every $10 rise could cut GDP by 44 bps and raise inflation by 91 bps.

Fiscal pressure may rise from lower excise collections and higher subsidies, but support is likely via guarantees rather than cash transfers.

OBITUARY • Chris Kornelis

Nobel-winning economist who upended the way we view inflation

Edmund S Phelps, awarded the Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences in 2006 for work that upended conventional wisdom about inflation and unemployment, died Friday at his Manhattan home. He was 92. His wife Viviana said the cause was Alzheimer's.

In his landmark 1968 paper "Money-Wage Dynamics and Labor-Market Equilibrium," Phelps argued that slowing the economy to keep inflation low might help unemployment in the short term, but over the long term stimulus would have to keep growing and inflation would keep rising.

He introduced the concept of the equilibrium unemployment rate – some unemployment is necessary in a healthy economy.

Central was his contention that expectations about inflation, particularly among businesses, mattered. "Whether you have high inflation or not depends largely upon the expectations of inflation on the players," he told The New York Times in 2023.

The Nobel committee noted: "Phelps showed how the possibilities of stabilisation policy in the future depend on today's policy decisions: Low inflation today leads to expectations of low inflation also in the future."

He reached similar conclusions to Milton Friedman around the same time. Harvard's Jason Furman said Phelps's message was more widely accepted because he was not seen as a partisan conservative.

Born July 26, 1933 in Evanston, Illinois, Phelps studied at Amherst (philosophy turned economics) and earned his PhD from Yale in 1959. He taught at Yale's Cowles Foundation and Columbia.

He authored more than two dozen books, including "Political Economy: An Introductory Text" (1985) and "Mass Flourishing" (2013). In 2024 he joined 15 other Nobel laureates warning about inflationary risks in a second Trump term.

"If you want to get on that highest consumption path in the long run, you don't want to save too little," he said, "because then you'll never get there."

OPINION • A K Bhattacharya • Raisina Hill

Not just an external threat

More attention needs to be paid to the fiscal implications of the West Asian conflict for Union and state governments.

With PM Modi outlining austerity steps, governments must plan for higher crude prices. India faces not just an external account problem but a grave fiscal challenge.

Austerity should conserve forex and help manage the external account. A widening current account deficit will require concerted efforts.

The fiscal challenge differs from an external crisis, though interrelated.

On revenue, the Centre cut special additional excise duty on petrol and diesel by ₹10/litre on March 26, costing over ₹1 trillion in FY27. States were spared as proceeds aren't shareable.

On expenditure, the fertiliser subsidy budgeted at ₹1.7 trillion is now set to rise to ₹2.41 trillion – an extra ₹70,000 crore.

Within days of the conflict, the finance ministry allocated ₹1 trillion to an Economic Stabilisation Fund. An earlier provision of ₹50,000 crore existed; the fund provides a cushion for global headwinds.

A credit guarantee scheme for MSMEs and airlines worth ₹2.55 trillion will cost about ₹18,000 crore.

Combined, additional expenditure and revenue loss is around ₹2.9 trillion. With nominal GDP around ₹393 trillion, this could widen the fiscal deficit by 0.73 percentage points from the budgeted 4.3% to well over 5%.

States are vulnerable. For 2025-26, 23 large states show a combined fiscal deficit over 3.3% of GSDP, with several above 3.5%: Andhra Pradesh 4.6%, Bihar 6.9%, Himachal 6%, Madhya Pradesh 4.6%, Rajasthan 3.8%, Telangana 4.36%.

Most depend heavily on central devolution – over half their own tax revenues. If central collections fall, states' balances will suffer first. Many may cut capital expenditure (already down to 2.2% of GSDP), hurting growth.

OP-ED • Narendra Modi & Giorgia Meloni

Italy and India: A strategic partnership for the Indo-Mediterranean

The relationship between Italy and India has reached a decisive stage, evolving into a strategic partnership grounded in freedom, democracy and a common vision.

In an uncertain international system, our partnership combines economic complementarities with millennia-old civilisational wisdom.

The EU-India FTA paves the way for trade. We aim to exceed €20 billion bilateral trade, focusing on defence, aerospace, clean tech, machinery, automotive, pharma, agri-food and tourism.

"Made in Italy" finds natural synergy with "Make in India". Over 1,000 companies operate in both directions.

Our cooperation builds on India's digital public infrastructure and Italy's industrial expertise. AI must be human-centric and serve the Global South. This vision underpinned Italy's G7 presidency and the AI Hub launched in New Delhi in 2025.

Security cooperation will cover maritime routes, terrorism, organised crime and trafficking.

Physical, digital and human connectivity links the Indo-Pacific and Mediterranean into an "Indo-Mediterranean" corridor. The India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) embodies this vision.

Energy is key – green hydrogen, smart grids, resilient infrastructure. Italy brings advanced renewables tech; India offers scale.

Guided by "Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam" and Italy's humanist tradition, we aim to place people at the centre of this partnership.
